title: UNDERSTANDING HTML WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS

structured.summary: This paper investigates the effectiveness of fine-tuning off-the-shelf pretrained large language models (LLMs) on raw HTML text for multiple HTML understanding tasks, including semantic classification, description generation, and autonomous web navigation. It demonstrates that encoder-decoder LLMs, particularly T5-based models, achieve strong performance and sample efficiency without specialized architectures or heavy preprocessing, bridging natural language pretraining with web automation applications.

strengths.point: Demonstrates strong performance of pretrained LLMs on diverse HTML understanding tasks without specialized architectures
strengths.evidence: Fine-tuned T5-3B achieves 51.8% success on MiniWoB navigation, outperforming prior supervised models by 16% with 192× less data; semantic classification accuracy improved by over 12% compared to training from scratch.

strengths.point: Introduces a new large-scale, auto-labeled dataset for description generation from CommonCrawl
strengths.evidence: 85K examples distilled and released publicly to encourage further research in HTML description generation.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical analysis of model architectures, sizes, and pretraining effects
strengths.evidence: Shows encoder-decoder models with bidirectional attention outperform encoder-only and decoder-only models; pretraining on natural language corpora improves performance up to 4.5×; model scaling yields sub-linear gains.

strengths.point: Minimal preprocessing approach using raw HTML tokenization and snippet extraction
strengths.evidence: Uses element-centric snippet extraction to fit HTML into context windows without complex structural encoding, simplifying pipeline.

weaknesses.point: Lacks comparison to recent HTML-specific architectures that explicitly model DOM structure
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation against graph neural networks or tree transformers designed for HTML, weakening novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient ablation and justification of snippet extraction and preprocessing choices
weaknesses.evidence: No detailed analysis on snippet size sensitivity or alternative hierarchical encoding methods.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation baselines and metrics are incomplete or potentially unfair
weaknesses.evidence: Unclear if prior navigation models use same input modalities or data; description generation metrics lack human or downstream task evaluation.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility hindered by missing hyperparameter details and sparse dataset release information
weaknesses.evidence: No fine-tuning hyperparameters or tokenization specifics provided; dataset release format and access details are sparse.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on input HTML preprocessing and real-time efficiency constraints
weaknesses.evidence: Ambiguous definition of 'raw HTML' input; no analysis of inference speed or computational cost for practical deployment.

weaknesses.point: No analysis of error types, failure modes, or statistical significance testing
weaknesses.evidence: Lacks qualitative error analysis and confidence intervals for reported accuracy gains.

weaknesses.point: No comparison to zero-shot or few-shot prompting baselines with large decoder-only LLMs
weaknesses.evidence: Misses important contemporary baselines given recent advances in prompting for HTML tasks.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation limited to English and shopping domains, limiting generalizability
weaknesses.evidence: No multilingual or diverse domain evaluation presented.

weaknesses.point: Novelty claims overlap with prior work on LLMs for web automation without clear differentiation
weaknesses.evidence: Prior works have explored LLMs for web navigation and form filling; paper does not clearly delineate unique contributions.

questions.question: How do pretrained LLMs compare to specialized HTML-structure-aware models in terms of sample efficiency and performance?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify whether general LLM fine-tuning suffices or specialized architectures remain necessary.

questions.question: What is the impact of different snippet extraction strategies or hierarchical encoding on model performance and context window limitations?
questions.motivation: Exploring this could improve handling of large, complex web pages beyond current snippet-based truncation.

questions.question: How well do these models generalize to non-English languages and diverse web domains beyond shopping sites?
questions.motivation: Assessing generalizability is critical for broader applicability of HTML understanding models.

questions.question: Can zero-shot or few-shot prompting with large decoder-only LLMs match or exceed fine-tuned model performance on HTML tasks?
questions.motivation: This would inform the necessity and cost-effectiveness of fine-tuning versus prompting approaches.

questions.question: What are the practical inference speed and computational resource requirements for deploying these models in real-time web automation?
questions.motivation: Efficiency considerations are essential for real-world applications where latency and cost matter.

critique_points.point_001: Lacks comparison to recent HTML-specific architectures that explicitly model DOM structure. No evaluation against graph neural networks or tree transformers designed for HTML, weakening novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient ablation and justification of snippet extraction and preprocessing choices. No detailed analysis on snippet size sensitivity or alternative hierarchical encoding methods
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation baselines and metrics are incomplete or potentially unfair. Unclear if prior navigation models use same input modalities or data; description generation metrics lack human or downstream task evaluation
critique_points.point_004: Reproducibility hindered by missing hyperparameter details and sparse dataset release information. No fine-tuning hyperparameters or tokenization specifics provided; dataset release format and access details are sparse
critique_points.point_005: Limited discussion on input HTML preprocessing and real-time efficiency constraints. Ambiguous definition of 'raw HTML' input; no analysis of inference speed or computational cost for practical deployment
critique_points.point_006: No analysis of error types, failure modes, or statistical significance testing. Lacks qualitative error analysis and confidence intervals for reported accuracy gains
critique_points.point_007: No comparison to zero-shot or few-shot prompting baselines with large decoder-only LLMs. Misses important contemporary baselines given recent advances in prompting for HTML tasks
critique_points.point_008: Evaluation limited to English and shopping domains, limiting generalizability. No multilingual or diverse domain evaluation presented
critique_points.point_009: Novelty claims overlap with prior work on LLMs for web automation without clear differentiation. Prior works have explored LLMs for web navigation and form filling; paper does not clearly delineate unique contributions
